title: Numbers 0 to 10
objective: You can understand and say the digits needed for age, room numbers, and basic counting.

teachingFrame.openingHook: Open with a room number said too quickly at a hotel desk, then show how knowing just eleven short Thai words solves the problem immediately.
teachingFrame.scenario: You need to catch digits in real life — room numbers, ages, queue numbers — and repeat them back clearly without freezing.
teachingFrame.learnerTakeaway: By the end, you can hear, repeat, and produce Thai digits 0-10 in room-number, counting, and simple age exchanges.

knownVocabulary.thai: คุณชื่ออะไร

knownGrammar: Teach the core greeting and polite endings ครับ/ค่ะ.
Teach courtesy phrases ขอบคุณ, ขอโทษ, ไม่เป็นไร with social-job framing.
Teach the name question and answer pattern คุณชื่ออะไร / ฉันชื่อ...
Teach the origin pattern มาจาก with country names.
Teach pronouns ฉัน, ผม, คุณ, เขา as tools for basic interaction.
Teach identity patterns with คือ and the who-question ใคร.

sample.thai: คุณชื่ออะไร

sections.heading: The first six digits: 0 to 5
sections.purpose: Give you a clean starting map for zero through five and show that Thai codes are usually read digit by digit.
spokenNarration: Let's start with the first six digits you will hear constantly in Thailand. Listen first: ศูนย์ sǔun, หนึ่ง nùeng, สอง sǎawng, สาม sǎam, สี่ sìi, ห้า hâa.
Now say them with me: ศูนย์, หนึ่ง, สอง, สาม, สี่, ห้า. Good.

One important thing. For room numbers, phone numbers, and queue numbers, Thai usually says each digit separately. Room 502 is not five-hundred-and-two. It is ห้า-ศูนย์-สอง. Digit by digit, like a code.
Notice that สี่ sìi has a low tone — your voice stays flat and low. Do not lift it up. If you say sǐi with a rising tone, that sounds like the word for colour, not the number four.
drills: Listen and repeat three times: ศูนย์-หนึ่ง-สอง-สาม-สี่-ห้า. Then say the same line backwards: ห้า-สี่-สาม-สอง-หนึ่ง-ศูนย์.
Your turn: I say a digit in English. You say the Thai word aloud before I show the answer. Ready? Two. Four. Zero. Five. One. Three.

languageFocus.notes: Falling tone. In faster speech this may reduce, but keep the full careful form for now.
Low tone. Do not confuse with สี sǐi (colour), which uses a rising tone.

visualPlan.onScreenGoal: Show the first six digits as a clean memory set with large Thai script and Arabic numerals.
teachingVisuals: Large stacked cards for 0-5 with Thai script, transliteration, and Arabic numeral grouped together on the left.
A simple code-reading line showing ห้า-ศูนย์-สอง = room 502 read digit by digit.
teacherCues: Pause after each digit and let the learner echo before moving on.
Point to the Arabic numeral only after the learner has heard the Thai form.

imageSupport.rationale: The digits themselves are the teaching target, so a photograph would add clutter without helping recall.

sections.heading: Finish the set: 6 to 10
sections.purpose: Teach digits 6-10 with listen-repeat-use, then practise the full 0-10 sequence in real scenarios like queue numbers and floors.
spokenNarration: Now add five more: หก hòk, เจ็ด jèt, แปด bpàet, เก้า gâo, สิบ sìp. Listen first: หก, เจ็ด, แปด, เก้า, สิบ.
Say them with me: หก, เจ็ด, แปด, เก้า, สิบ. Good. Now count the full set from zero: ศูนย์, หนึ่ง, สอง, สาม, สี่, ห้า, หก, เจ็ด, แปด, เก้า, สิบ.
Let's use them. Imagine you are at a hospital. The screen says queue number เจ็ด. That is you — number seven. Or you are in a building and someone says floor หก. That is floor six.
Now a quick scenario: someone asks what floor? You need floor nine. Say เก้า. What about floor three? สาม. Floor ten? สิบ.
drills: Listen and repeat: หก, เจ็ด, แปด, เก้า, สิบ. Now count the full set 0-10 without looking.
Pause-and-produce: I say a number in English. You say the Thai word before I show it. Seven. Ten. Eight. Six. Nine.
Substitution drill: what floor? I say a number, you say the Thai digit. Floor 9? เก้า. Floor 6? หก. Floor 10? สิบ.

languageFocus.notes: Short vowel, clipped final. Do not stretch.
Short vowel with a sharp -t final stop.
Unaspirated bp- onset. No puff of air.

visualPlan.onScreenGoal: Show digits 6-10 as a clean set matching the section 1 layout for 0-5.
teachingVisuals: Large stacked cards for 6-10 with Thai script, transliteration, and Arabic numeral.
Below: a mini queue-number scenario showing a number on a screen.
teacherCues: Model each digit naturally, then use in a floor/queue scenario.
Have the learner count the full 0-10 set from memory.

imageSupport.rationale: Digit cards with Arabic numerals are the clearest teaching tool. No stock photos needed.

sections.heading: Ask for a number and say your age
sections.purpose: Introduce the two practical chunks เบอร์อะไร and ฉันอายุ...ปี so digits become immediately usable in real exchanges.
spokenNarration: Now make the digits useful. First, a question you will hear everywhere: เบอร์อะไร ber à-rai — what number? You hear this with rooms, tables, phones, and queue tickets. เบอร์ is a borrowed word for a number label.
Listen and repeat: เบอร์อะไรครับ. เบอร์อะไรค่ะ. Good. Now answer: someone asks เบอร์อะไร and the room is 307. You say สาม-ศูนย์-เจ็ด. Digit by digit.
Next, the age pattern. You already know ฉัน and เขา from the last lesson. Now add: ฉันอายุ...ปี chǎn aa-yú ... bpii — I am ... years old. Drop any number into the slot. ฉันอายุห้าปี. ฉันอายุสิบปี.
Notice there is no word for am in the Thai pattern. You do not say I am five years old. You say I age five year. The pattern is: pronoun, then อายุ, then the number, then ปี.
onScreenBullets: เบอร์อะไร | ber à-rai | what number?
ฉันอายุ...ปี | chǎn aa-yú ... bpii | I am ... years old
เขาอายุ...ปี | khǎo aa-yú ... bpii | he/she is ... years old
drills: Prompt drill: I ask เบอร์อะไร. You answer with a digit string. Room 205? สอง-ศูนย์-ห้า. Room 409? สี่-ศูนย์-เก้า. Your turn.
Pattern drill: I give you a number. You build the age sentence. Five? ฉันอายุห้าปี. Eight? ฉันอายุแปดปี. Ten? ฉันอายุสิบปี.
Substitution drill: now change the pronoun. I say a number and a pronoun. เขา, seven? เขาอายุเจ็ดปี. ผม, nine? ผมอายุเก้าปี.

languageFocus.notes: เบอร์ is borrowed from English 'number' but narrowed to label-type numbers: rooms, phones, tables, queues.
No verb 'to be' in the Thai pattern. Pronoun + อายุ + number + ปี.

visualPlan.onScreenGoal: Anchor digit use in a real-world number context and show the age pattern as a fill-in frame.
teachingVisuals: Room-number example: สาม-ศูนย์-เจ็ด with each digit spoken separately, anchored by a room-sign photo.
Age pattern frame: [pronoun] + อายุ + [___] + ปี with an open slot for the number.
teacherCues: Keep the room-number example large on the left, photo as subtle background.
Show the age pattern as a sentence frame with a visible slot, not as a finished sentence.

imageSupport.rationale: A real hotel room-number plaque makes the use case instantly concrete and shows why Thai digits are said one by one.
searchQueries: Thailand hotel room number door plaque

sections.heading: Numbers in real exchanges
sections.purpose: Move from isolated practice into short exchanges that combine numbers with known pronouns, courtesy phrases, and the age pattern, recycling prior material.
spokenNarration: Now put everything together. You already know สวัสดี for greeting, ขอบคุณ for thanking, and ขอโทษ for getting attention. You know ฉัน, ผม, คุณ, and เขา for talking about people. Now numbers plug straight into that system.
Imagine someone asks คุณอายุเท่าไหร่ — how old are you? You answer: ฉันอายุแปดปี. Or ผมอายุสิบปี. Same pattern, different pronoun, different number.
If you miss a number, do not freeze. Say ขอโทษครับ or ขอโทษค่ะ, then listen again. A repair phrase keeps the exchange moving.
Let's practise a short exchange. I ask เบอร์อะไร. You answer with a room number. Then I ask อายุเท่าไหร่. You answer with your age pattern. Say your lines before I show the answer.
onScreenBullets: คุณอายุเท่าไหร่ | khun aa-yú thâo-rài | how old are you?
ฉันอายุแปดปี | chǎn aa-yú bpàet bpii | I am eight years old
ขอโทษครับ / ค่ะ | khǎaw-thôot khráp / khâ | excuse me (polite)
drills: Response-building drill: I ask เบอร์อะไรครับ. You answer with room 601. Then I ask คุณอายุเท่าไหร่. You answer with age seven. Say your lines before looking.
Repair drill: I say a three-digit room number once, fast. If you catch it, repeat it back. If you miss it, say ขอโทษครับ or ขอโทษค่ะ, then I repeat and you try again.
Substitution drill: change the person. เขาอายุสามปี. Now change the number: เขาอายุหกปี. Now change both: ฉันอายุสิบปี.

visualPlan.onScreenGoal: Show combined exchanges that use digits with pronouns and courtesy phrases as a unified system.
teachingVisuals: A compact exchange ladder: question on top, answer below, with the number slot highlighted.
A repair mini-dialogue showing ขอโทษ before the number is repeated.
teacherCues: Tie the age pattern back to pronouns from lesson 3 explicitly.
Keep the repair drill moving quickly — the point is that ขอโทษ buys time.

imageSupport.rationale: This section is about combining known material with new numbers, so a clean drill stack teaches better than imagery.

sections.heading: Pronunciation beat: low tone vs rising tone
sections.purpose: Train your ear on the low tone vs rising tone contrast that changes meaning in this lesson's vocabulary, specifically สี่ (four) vs สี (colour).
spokenNarration: Before the roleplay, let's sharpen your ear. Thai tones change meaning. You have been practising this since lesson one with ครับ and ค่ะ. Today the key contrast is low tone versus rising tone.

Listen: สี่ sìi — that is a low tone. Your voice stays flat and low, like a calm, steady statement. Now listen: สี sǐi — that is a rising tone. Your voice lifts up from low to high, like asking really? in English.
สี่ with a low tone means four. สี with a rising tone means colour. Wrong tone, wrong word. Try it. Say สี่ sìi — keep it low and flat. Now say สี sǐi — lift it up.

Here is another contrast from this lesson. เก้า gâo — falling tone, means nine. เข้า khâo — also a falling tone, but different first sound. For now, just notice that the tone on เก้า drops down firmly, like saying no with conviction.
onScreenBullets: สี่ | sìi | four (low tone — voice stays low and flat)
สี | sǐi | colour (rising tone — voice lifts up)
เก้า | gâo | nine (falling tone — voice drops down)
drills: Tone echo: I say สี่ sìi. Repeat it with a low tone. Now I say สี sǐi. Repeat it with a rising tone. Can you feel the difference?
Minimal pair choice: I say one word. Is it four or colour? Point to the answer, then say the word back to me.

languageFocus.notes: Pronunciation focus: low tone stays flat and low. Contrast with สี sǐi (colour, rising tone).

visualPlan.onScreenGoal: Show tone direction arrows next to the transliteration to visualize pitch movement for low vs rising.
teachingVisuals: Two large cards: สี่ sìi with a flat-low arrow, สี sǐi with an upward arrow.
Simple pitch contour line showing low tone as flat-low and rising tone as lifting up.
teacherCues: Model each tone slowly and clearly, exaggerating the difference.
Use hand gestures: flat palm for low tone, palm lifting for rising tone.

imageSupport.rationale: Tone arrows and pitch contour lines teach pronunciation better than any photograph.

roleplay.scenario: You are checking in at a small hotel. You need to get your room number and give your age for a registration form.

lines.speaker: You
lines.thai: สวัสดีครับ

lines.speaker: Staff
lines.thai: สวัสดีค่ะ ห้องเบอร์อะไรคะ
lines.translit: sà-wàt-dii khâ hâawng ber à-rai khá
lines.english: Hello. What is the room number?

lines.speaker: You
lines.thai: ห้า-ศูนย์-สองครับ

lines.speaker: Staff
lines.thai: ค่ะ คุณอายุเท่าไหร่คะ

lines.speaker: You
lines.thai: ผมอายุสิบปีครับ

lines.speaker: Staff
lines.thai: สิบปีค่ะ ขอบคุณค่ะ

lines.speaker: You
lines.thai: ขอบคุณครับ

recap: How do you ask 'what number?' เบอร์อะไร.
Say 'I am 8 years old' in Thai.
สี่ has a low tone. What happens with a rising tone?

targetSounds: short clipped finals (hòk, jèt, bpàet) vs longer open vowels (hâa, gâo)
pronunciationFocus.mouthMapAnchor: Low tone: keep your voice flat and low, like a calm, steady statement. Rising tone: your voice lifts up from low to high, like asking 'really?' in English.
